2.32: A fulfillment: 'And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved.' Peter (Acts 2:21) and Paul (Rom 10:13) both cite Joel 2:32 as fulfilled in the gospel proclamation: the 'name of the LORD' whose invocation brings salvation is now identified as Jesus, whom God has made both Lord and Christ (Acts 2:36). The typological identification is clear: YHWH's name in the OT = Jesus's name in the NT. This is not a revision of the OT but the NT's claim that YHWH's name and Jesus's name are the same divine identity now disclosed fully in the incarnation. The universal accessibility of salvation ('everyone who calls') removes the national and ethnic boundaries that had characterized the OT covenant community.
2.1: A direct revelation: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain!' The shofar at Zion signals both alarm and assembly. Matt 24:31: he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds. 1 Cor 15:52: at the last trumpet ... the dead will be raised imperishable. Rev 11:15: at the sound of the seventh trumpet the kingdom of the world becomes the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ. Joel's trumpet-blast at Zion is the OT template for the eschatological trumpet that announces the arrival of the Son of Man — the alarm that wakes the dead and gathers the living.
2.2: A shadow: 'A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness. Like the dawn spreading over the mountains a great and powerful people.' The darkness imagery of the Day of the LORD appears at the crucifixion: Mark 15:33 — at the sixth hour darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour. The cross is the Day of the LORD in concentrated form — the wrath that was to come fell on the Son. The darkness at noon was not eclipse but divine sign: the day of cosmic judgment had arrived, not in its final eschatological form but in its redemptive concentrated form, as the Lord himself bore the darkness for those who would have faced it finally.
2.3: A shadow: 'Before them a fire devours, and behind them a flame burns. Before them the land is like the garden of Eden, and behind them a desolate wilderness.' The reversal of Eden — the garden destroyed by the divine army's advance — reflects the trajectory of the fall and the trajectory of redemption. Behind the army of judgment is desolation; but ahead of the new creation restored by Christ is the garden again: Rev 22:1–2 (the river of the water of life ... the tree of life ... for the healing of the nations). The fire that leaves Eden-waste behind it is the same fire that Christ came to cast on the earth (Luke 12:49); both fires serve the ultimate restoration of the garden.
2.4: A shadow: 'Their appearance is like that of horses; and like war horses, so they charge.' Revelation 9:7 directly borrows this image: the locusts from the abyss were like horses prepared for battle. Rev 19:11 inverts it: the Rider on the white horse leads the armies of heaven — Christ at his parousia commands the army that Joel's locust-imagery had previewed. The locust-horses of ch2 are the type of the judgment armies; the white-horse Rider is the one who both leads the final judgment and has already absorbed its central blow at the cross.
2.5: A shadow: 'Like the rumbling of chariots they leap over the mountaintops, like the crackling of a flame of fire consuming stubble.' Revelation 9:9 (the sound of their wings was like the sound of many chariots with horses rushing into battle) is a direct citation. The unstoppable sound-advance of the divine army is the type of the unstoppable mission of the gospel: Rom 10:18 (their voice has gone out to all the earth, and their words to the ends of the world). The chariots of judgment become the chariots of proclamation when the Judge becomes the Savior.
2.6: A shadow: 'Before them peoples writhe in anguish; every face turns pale.' Rev 6:15–17: the kings of the earth and the great ones and the generals and the rich and the powerful hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains, calling to the mountains, 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who is seated on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb.' The paleness of Joel's peoples before the approaching army is the OT preview of the terror of the Lamb's wrath in Revelation — the same divine face that the covenant people welcome is the face before which the unrepentant flee.
2.7: A shadow: 'Like warriors they run; like soldiers they scale the wall. Each one keeps to his own course; they do not swerve from their paths.' The disciplined advance of the divine army — each in its assigned course, nothing deviating — is the type of the irresistible completion of the divine plan. God's purposes do not deviate or fail: Isa 46:10–11 (I will accomplish all my purpose); John 19:30 (it is finished — tetelestai, the complete execution of the plan). The divine army that keeps its course perfectly is the shadow of the Son who kept his course perfectly from Bethlehem to the empty tomb.
2.8: A shadow: 'They do not push each other; each advances in his own track. They burst through the weapons without halting; they do not swerve from their paths.' The weapons-resistant advance of the locust army is the type of the unstoppable progress of Christ's mission. John 10:18: no one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. The cross was not an interruption of the divine plan but its execution: not the weapons of the enemy stopping God's advance but Christ willingly walking into them. Luke 9:51: he set his face to go to Jerusalem — the same resolute advance that Joel's army models.
2.9: A shadow: 'They rush through the city; they run along the walls; they climb up into the houses; they enter through the windows like a thief.' The comprehensive penetration of the city — no hiding place, no sealed room — is the type of the inescapable reach of divine judgment. Rev 3:20: behold, I stand at the door and knock — the same entry-through-every-opening, but now as the invitation of grace rather than the enforcement of judgment. For those who welcome the entry: Rev 3:20 (I will come in to him and eat with him). For those who resist: the inescapable nature of the final reckoning (Rev 20:12: the dead were judged by what was written in the books).
2.10: A direct revelation: 'Before them the earth quakes, the heavens tremble; the sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The cosmic shaking at the approach of the divine army is cited in Heb 12:26–27: at that time his voice shook the earth, but now he has promised, 'Yet once more I will shake not only the earth but also the heavens.' This means the removal of things that are shaken in order that the things that cannot be shaken may remain. Christ's parousia will be this final, comprehensive cosmic shaking — the undoing of the old creation's framework to make room for the new. The dark sun and moon appear at the cross (Mark 15:33) as the preview shaking.
2.11: A direct revelation: 'The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his forces are exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful.' The LORD speaking before his army is the type of the creative-commanding Word who leads the final judgment. Rev 19:15: from his mouth comes a sharp sword with which to strike down the nations. The word of Christ that once said 'Let there be light' and 'Lazarus, come out' is the same word that will one day say 'Depart' and 'Come, blessed of my Father' (Matt 25:34, 41). Joel's LORD commanding his army is the God who in Christ exercises this command with both mercy and judgment.
2.12: A direct revelation: 'Yet even now, declares the LORD, return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.' The invitation in the very midst of announced judgment — 'yet even now' (vegam attah) — is the grace-logic of the entire gospel. Matt 11:28: come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Luke 15:20: while he was still a long way off, his father saw him. The 'yet even now' of Joel is the permanent posture of Christ toward the returning: not 'it is too late' but 'even now, return.' 2 Pet 3:9: the Lord is patient, not wishing that any should perish but that all should reach repentance.
2.13: A direct revelation: 'Rend your hearts and not your garments. Return to the LORD your God, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and he relents over disaster.' This verse contains the self-description of YHWH from Exod 34:6–7 — the great covenant formula. Christ is the embodiment of this character: John 1:14 (the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth — the NT equivalent of chesed ve-emet). The demand for inward rather than external religion (rend your hearts, not garments) is Jesus's consistent teaching (Matt 5:3–8; 23:27–28). The God who relents over disaster does not relent arbitrarily but because Christ has absorbed the disaster in his body on the tree.
2.14: A shadow: 'Who knows? He may turn and relent and leave behind a blessing — a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God.' The 'who knows?' of Joel's penitential appeal is the OT's honest acknowledgment that mercy is not owed but hoped for. 2 Pet 3:9: the Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish. In Christ, the 'who knows?' is answered: it is YHWH's revealed will that all should come to repentance. The grain offering and drink offering that might be left behind — bread and wine — are the very elements Christ designated as the permanent memorial of his body and blood (1 Cor 11:25).
2.15: A type: 'Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.' The second trumpet-call in ch2 (cf. v1) shifts from alarm to assembly. The assembled community before God is the type of the church gathered in Christ's name. Matt 18:20: where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them. The solemn assembly of Joel that calls the people to repentance prefigures the NT assembly (ekklesia) that gathers in the name of the risen Christ. The fast that Joel calls finds its NT fulfillment in the fasting that Christ anticipated his disciples would do after his departure (Matt 9:15: the days will come when the bridegroom is taken from them, and then they will fast).
2.16: A type: 'Gather the people; consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children and nursing infants. Let the bridegroom leave his room, and the bride her chamber.' The inclusive scope of the assembly — elders to nursing infants — prefigures the new covenant's comprehensive invitation. Matt 19:14: let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven. The bridegroom leaving his chamber at the assembly's most urgent moment is the type of Christ's parousia: the Bridegroom who is coming (Matt 25:6: 'the bridegroom is coming!') leaves the heavenly chamber to meet his bride.
2.17: A type: 'Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests weep and say, Spare your people, O LORD, and make not your heritage a reproach.' The priests standing between the vestibule and the altar — the innermost accessible space of the temple — are the type of Christ's high-priestly intercession. Heb 7:25: he always lives to make intercession for them. Heb 9:24: Christ has entered into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf. The priestly prayer 'spare your people' is what Christ permanently offers in the heavenly sanctuary on behalf of those who are in him. The prayer of Joel's priests became Christ's permanent intercession.
2.18: A direct revelation: 'Then the LORD became zealous for his land and had compassion on his people.' The divine jealousy/zeal (qanna) for his land and his people is the covenantal passion that drives the entire redemptive mission. John 2:17: his disciples remembered that it is written, 'Zeal for your house will consume me.' Christ's cleansing of the temple is the acting-out of YHWH's zealous love for his dwelling place and his people. The compassion that follows the assembly and fasting of vv12–17 is the template for the Father's response to the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20: his father saw him ... and ran and embraced him and kissed him).
2.19: A direct revelation: 'The LORD answered and said to his people: Behold, I am sending you grain, wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied.' The divine response to the penitential assembly: provision of grain, wine, and oil. These three elements — the staples of the covenant land — are also the elements of the Eucharist in symbolic form: grain (bread) and wine as Christ's body and blood (1 Cor 11:24–25), and oil as the anointing of the Spirit (Acts 10:38: God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit). The restoration of what the locust took is also the provision of the three liturgical elements that Christ designates as the memorial of his sacrifice.
2.20: A shadow: 'I will drive the northern invader far from you, into a parched and desolate land.' The expulsion of the adversarial northern army into permanent desolation is the type of the final defeat of the adversary. Rev 20:10: the devil who had deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur. The northern invader whose stench rises to heaven is destroyed permanently — not just pushed back temporarily but consigned to desolation. Christ's victory at the cross (Col 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities) begins this expulsion that is consummated at the parousia.
2.21: A direct revelation: 'Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things.' The fear-not oracle following the defeat of the locust-army is the Easter proclamation in miniature. Luke 1:49: he who is mighty has done great things for me (megaleia). Acts 2:11: we hear them telling in our own tongues the mighty works of God (megalia tou Theou). The angel's announcement to the women at the tomb: Do not be afraid (Matt 28:5). The structure is Joel's: (1) judgment comes; (2) assembly and repentance; (3) YHWH acts; (4) fear not, great things have been done. The resurrection is the supreme great thing YHWH has done, fulfilling the pattern Joel established.
2.22: A shadow: 'Fear not, you wild animals, for the pastures of the wilderness are green again; the trees are bearing their fruit; the fig tree and vine give their full yield.' The restoration of creation — even the wild animals reassured — is the beginning of what Rom 8:21 calls the 'freedom of the glory of the children of God' into which the whole creation will be released. The fear of the animals in a barren creation (v18) gives way to a flourishing in which animal life participates. Isa 11:6–9 and Rev 21–22 extend this vision: the new creation's fruitfulness encompasses not just human redemption but the restoration of the entire natural order.
2.23: A direct revelation: 'Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain for your vindication (moreh litzedaqah).' The Hebrew moreh litzedaqah can mean both 'early rain for righteousness' (agricultural reading) and 'teacher of righteousness' (sapiential reading). The early rain in late autumn (October–November) is the soil-softening rain that makes planting possible; the latter rain (April–May) fills the grain before harvest. As 'teacher of righteousness,' the phrase was read mesianically in Dead Sea Scrolls texts. Whether or not Joel intends the messianic reading, Christ as the one who gives both the Spirit (the rain of the new covenant) and is himself the Teacher of righteousness (John 13:13–14) fulfills both senses.
2.24: A direct revelation: 'The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.' The overflowing abundance — threshing floors and vats of wine and oil — is the eschatological harvest imagery that runs through both Testaments. Rev 14:14–16: the Son of Man sits on a white cloud with a golden crown, and an angel cries 'Put in your sickle, and reap, for the hour to reap has come, for the harvest of the earth is fully ripe.' The grain and wine that overflow Joel's restored land are the same grain and wine that Jesus designated at the Last Supper as his body and blood — the harvest of his own life poured out for many (Mark 14:24).
2.25: A direct revelation: 'I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten — the hopping locust, the stripping locust, and the cutting locust, my great army which I sent among you.' The restoration of what was consumed — the years themselves — is the OT's most radical promise of redemption: not just recovery of property but recovery of time. Eph 5:16: making the best use of the time (exagorazomenoi ton kairon, redeeming the time). Christ's redemption reaches backward to restore what was lost: the resurrection ensures that nothing good is ultimately lost. Rev 21:4–5: he will wipe away every tear from their eyes ... behold, I am making all things new — including the years the locust ate.
2.26: A direct revelation: 'You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God, who has dealt wondrously with you.' The eating to satisfaction and praising is the description of the messianic feast. Rev 19:9: blessed are those who are invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb. The praise of YHWH's name follows from the great deeds done for his people: Luke 1:49 (Mary's Magnificat: he who is mighty has done great things for me, and holy is his name). The satisfaction of ch2:26 is the satisfaction Christ offers (John 6:35: whoever comes to me shall not hunger) — permanent, not subject to the locust's return.
2.27: A direct revelation: 'You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else.' The divine presence in the midst of Israel is the covenant promise (Exod 29:45: I will dwell among the people of Israel and will be their God). John 14:20: in that day you will know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you. Matt 1:23: Emmanuel — God with us. The knowledge that YHWH is in the midst of his people is fulfilled first in the incarnation (God bodily present among his people), then through the Spirit (John 14:17: the Spirit of truth ... dwells with you and will be in you), and finally in the new creation (Rev 21:3: the dwelling of God is with man).
2.28: A direct fulfillment: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' Peter explicitly cites this as fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:16–17: this is what was uttered through the prophet Joel). The universal Spirit-gift breaks down the structures that had limited prophetic access: not just prophets and kings but sons and daughters, old and young, male and female servants (v29). Christ is the baptizer with the Holy Spirit (Matt 3:11: he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire) — the outpouring of Joel 2:28 occurs because Christ has been exalted and poured out the Spirit (Acts 2:33).
2.29: A direct fulfillment: 'Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.' The extension of the Spirit to slaves is the social revolution of the new covenant. Gal 3:28: there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus. Acts 2:18: even on my male servants and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit. The social hierarchy that excluded servants from the prophetic vocation is dissolved by the Spirit's universal outpouring — a direct consequence of Christ's exaltation: Acts 2:33 (being therefore exalted at the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing).
2.30: A shadow: 'And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.' The cosmic signs — blood, fire, smoke — appeared in concentrated form at the crucifixion: darkness over the land (Mark 15:33), the veil torn (Mark 15:38), the earthquake (Matt 27:51). These are the inauguration of the Day-of-the-LORD signs that Joel announces. The final fulfillment is the parousia: Rev 8:5–7 (thunder, lightning, earthquake, hail and fire mixed with blood). Every sign Joel announces previews either the cross or the parousia — Christ's two advents are the two poles of the Day-of-the-LORD's fulfillment.
2.31: A direct revelation: 'The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' Jesus cites this directly (Matt 24:29: immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light; Mark 13:24). Rev 6:12: when he opened the sixth seal, the sun became black as sackcloth and the full moon became like blood. Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:20) cites this verse as part of the Joel 2:28–32 prophecy being fulfilled — the cosmic signs are the eschatological frame within which the Spirit's outpouring takes place. The sun-to-darkness at the crucifixion (Mark 15:33) is the historical pre-fulfillment; the parousia-darkness is the final fulfillment.

1.1: A type and introduction: 'The word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel.' Joel's prophetic vocation — receiving and transmitting the divine word — is a type of the incarnate Word. Every OT prophet who spoke 'the word of the LORD' was an imperfect vessel for the communication that would be made perfectly in Christ (Heb 1:1–2: in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son). Joel's word is notable for its double horizon: the present locust crisis and the eschatological Day of the LORD are linked, just as Christ's first advent and his parousia are linked in the NT's 'already and not yet' structure.
1.2: A shadow: 'Has anything like this ever happened in your days?' Joel's call to the elders frames the locust plague as an unprecedented event that demands intergenerational memory. The 'unprecedented' character of Joel's plague prefigures the unprecedented character of the Day of the LORD — and of the cross itself. At Calvary, the disciples asked whether anything like this had ever happened: the Son of God crucified, darkness over the land, the temple veil torn. The answer in both testaments: no, nothing like this has ever happened. The uniqueness of Joel's crisis points forward to the unique, unrepeatable event of the incarnation and atonement.
1.3: A type: 'Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children, and their children to the next generation.' The intergenerational proclamation mandate mirrors the Passover command (Exod 12:26–27: when your children ask, 'What does this mean?' you shall say ...) and the Great Commission (Matt 28:19–20: go and make disciples of all nations ... teaching them to observe everything I have commanded). The locust event must be told and retold; likewise the death and resurrection of Christ are to be proclaimed until he comes (1 Cor 11:26: as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes).
1.4: A shadow: the four locusts — cutting, swarming, hopping, destroying — represent the exhaustive, comprehensive nature of the judgment that precedes the messianic restoration. Revelation 9:3–11 draws directly on Joel's locust imagery for the fifth-trumpet plague: locusts from the abyss, with power like scorpions, tormenting those without God's seal. The fourfold progression signals totality: nothing is left untouched. Christ's cross is the ultimate exhaustion of judgment — he bore the full weight of the covenant curse (Gal 3:13: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us) so that nothing remains for those who are in him.
1.5: A type: 'Wake up, you drunkards, and weep!' Joel's call to the wine-benumbed to wake from stupor and mourn uses the sleeping/waking vocabulary that Paul applies to eschatological alertness: 1 Thess 5:6–8 (let us not sleep as others do, but let us keep awake and be sober). The drunkards who do not see the locust crisis coming are the type of those who do not see the Day of the LORD approaching. Christ's parable of the sleeping virgins (Matt 25:5–13) uses the same contrast: those who sleep are unprepared for the Bridegroom's coming. The call to wake and weep is the precondition for the joy that follows in 2:21.
1.6: A shadow: 'A nation has invaded my land — powerful and beyond counting; its teeth are like lions' teeth, and it has the fangs of a lioness.' Revelation 9:8 borrows directly: the locust-army from the abyss has teeth like lions' teeth. The locust nation that invades YHWH's land is the type of the demonic forces that assault the covenant people — and ultimately of the spiritual powers of darkness that Christ defeated at the cross. Col 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him. The devouring nation is met by a greater conqueror.
1.7: A type: 'It has stripped my vine bare and splintered my fig tree.' The vine and the fig tree are Israel's primary covenant symbols (Mic 4:4; John 1:48). The stripped vine is the judgment that precedes the Vine's true revelation: Christ as the true vine (John 15:1–5: I am the true vine, and my Father is the vinedresser ... every branch that does not bear fruit he takes away). The cursing of the fig tree (Matt 21:19–20) and the cleansing of the temple are Jesus's enacted judgment on the stripped, fruitless institutions of the old covenant — the same judgment Joel describes, now carried out by the Lord of the vineyard himself.
1.8: A type: 'Mourn like a young woman dressed in sackcloth, grieving for the husband of her youth.' The widow mourning her young husband is the most acute image of irreversible loss in the OT. The bride-and-bridegroom vocabulary runs through the prophets (Hos 2:19–20; Isa 54:5–6) and culminates in the NT (John 3:29: the friend of the bridegroom rejoices greatly at the bridegroom's voice; Eph 5:25–27; Rev 19:7). The mourning of v8 is the church's experience in Christ's absence — the disciples mourned like those who had lost the bridegroom (Matt 9:15: can the wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them?). The mourning precedes and guarantees the wedding supper.
1.9: A shadow: 'The grain offering and drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD.' The cessation of the daily liturgy is the most grievous effect of the locust plague: worship is interrupted. This foreshadows the ultimate interruption — the destruction of the temple in 70 CE — and its theological resolution in Christ: the offering that was cut off is replaced by the one perfect offering. Heb 10:14: by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified. The grain offering and drink offering — bread and wine — find their deepest fulfillment in the Eucharist, where Christ gives himself as the permanent liturgy that no locust can interrupt.
1.10: A shadow: 'The fields are ruined; the soil is in mourning. The grain is destroyed, the new wine dries up, the olive oil fails.' The triple failure of grain, wine, and oil mirrors the covenant curse structure of Deut 28:38–42 (you shall plant a vineyard but not harvest it ... the locust shall devour all your trees). More profoundly, the ruined land is the type of the creation subjected to futility (Rom 8:20–22: the whole creation has been groaning in the pains of childbirth until now). The restoration of grain and wine and oil (Joel 2:19, 24) is the eschatological promise that Christ's redemption will reclaim all of creation, not merely individual souls.
1.11: A type: 'Be ashamed, O farmers; wail, O vinedressers — because the harvest of the field has perished.' The shame of those who worked but did not harvest is the type of the eschatological harvest failure for those who are not 'in the vine' (John 15:6: if anyone does not abide in me he is thrown away like a branch and withers). Conversely, those who abide in Christ bear fruit at the final harvest (Matt 13:30: gather the wheat into my barn). The shame of Joel's farmers who tended the crop but lost the harvest is reversed in the restored harvest of 2:24, grounded in the union with Christ who is himself the grain of wheat that fell into the earth and died (John 12:24).
1.12: A shadow: 'The vine has dried up and the fig tree withers — the pomegranate, the palm, and the apple tree — all the trees of the field are dried up, and joy has dried up from the children of man.' All seven trees and all joy are gone. The comprehensiveness of this desolation is the type of the spiritual barrenness that Christ confronts at his coming (Luke 18:8: when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?). But the withered vine and fig — the very symbols of Israel's covenant blessing — find their resurrection in Christ: he is the vine that does not wither (John 15:1), and those who are in him will never go thirsty (John 4:14).
1.13: A type: 'Put on sackcloth and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar. Come, spend the night in sackcloth, O ministers of my God.' The priests weeping at the altar is the OT's model of intercessory grief on behalf of the people. Heb 7:25: Christ lives to make intercession for the people — but his intercession is not one of sackcloth and weeping but of triumphant high priestly presentation of his own blood (Heb 9:12: he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption). Joel's weeping priests point forward to the one High Priest whose intercession never needs to be renewed.
1.14: A type: 'Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly. Gather the elders and all the inhabitants of the land to the house of the LORD your God, and cry out to the LORD.' The solemn assembly before YHWH in the face of catastrophe is the OT liturgical pattern that Christ fulfills in his gathering of all peoples to the Father. Matt 18:20: where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them. The fast that Joel commands is answered by Christ, who taught his disciples that prayer and fasting were the appropriate response to the powers that cannot be overcome otherwise (Mark 9:29). The assembly before the house of YHWH points to the new temple that Christ's body is (John 2:21).
1.15: A direct revelation: 'Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and it comes as devastation from the Almighty.' The Day of the LORD is the single most prominent eschatological theme in Joel — and in the NT, it becomes the Day of Christ. 1 Thess 5:2: the day of the Lord comes like a thief in the night. 2 Pet 3:10: the day of the Lord will come like a thief, and then the heavens will pass away. The Almighty who brings the Day in Joel is identified in Revelation as the Alpha and Omega (Rev 1:8: who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty — pantokrator). Christ is the one through whom the Day of the LORD is both announced and administered.
1.16: A shadow: 'Is not the food already cut off before our eyes, and joy and gladness from the house of our God?' The disappearance of food and joy from the house of YHWH is the inverse of the new covenant's eucharistic abundance. John 6:35: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst. The joy that was cut off from the house of God is restored in the Spirit (Gal 5:22: the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace ...), and the eucharistic feast is the anticipation of the wedding supper where abundance cannot be cut off (Rev 19:9).
1.17: A shadow: 'The seeds have shrivelled in the ground; the storehouses stand empty; the granaries are torn down, for the grain has dried up.' The dead seed in the ground is the precondition for the harvest of resurrection. John 12:24: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. Christ is the grain that fell into the earth — the death that appeared to be the final shriveling was actually the beginning of the harvest. The empty storehouses of Joel's crisis are transformed into the full threshing floors of 2:24 through the death and resurrection of the one true Seed.
1.18: A shadow: 'How the animals groan! The herds of cattle are confused because there is no pasture for them; even the flocks of sheep suffer.' The groaning of animals in Joel's crisis is the specific sound of what Paul describes in cosmic terms: Rom 8:22 — the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now. The animals who cannot find pasture are the type of all creation waiting for the liberation that comes with the revelation of the sons of God (Rom 8:19–21). Christ's restoration of creation — beginning with the resurrection, consummated in the new creation — is the answer to the groan of Joel's cattle.
1.19: A type: 'To you, O LORD, I cry out. For fire has devoured the open pastures and flame has burned all the trees of the field.' The prophet's prayer in the midst of consuming fire is the type of the intercession that the Spirit makes through the saints (Rom 8:26: the Spirit helps us in our weakness ... the Spirit himself intercedes for us with groanings too deep for words). The fire that devours the pastures is the covenant curse; the prayer that rises from that fire is the covenant appeal. Christ from the cross cried out (Matt 27:46: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?) — the deepest cry from within the consuming fire of divine wrath, heard and answered in the resurrection.
1.20: A shadow: 'Even the wild animals pant and cry to you, for the water brooks have dried up and fire has devoured the open pastures.' The panting of wild animals for dried-up water echoes Ps 42:1 (as a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God). The dried water brooks of Joel's crisis point to the Living Water that Christ offers: John 7:37–38 (if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink; out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — speaking of the Spirit). The panting of every creature for water in ch1 is answered in ch2's dew and rain (2:23) and fulfilled permanently in Rev 22:17 (the Spirit and the Bride say Come; let the one who is thirsty come).

3.1: A direct revelation: 'For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.' The restoration of fortunes (shuvti shevut) is the eschatological reversal formula of the prophets (Jer 30:3; Ezek 39:25; Zeph 3:20). Christ's resurrection is the beginning of this restoration: Acts 3:21 (heaven must receive him until the time for restoring all things). The 'those days' of Joel point to the new covenant age inaugurated at Pentecost (cf. Acts 2:17: 'in the last days') and consummated at the parousia. Christ is both the agent and the substance of the restoration — in him the fortunes of Zion are restored (Eph 1:3: blessed with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ).
3.2: A direct revelation: 'I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat, and I will enter into judgment with them there on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel.' The universal judgment of all nations is fulfilled in Christ. Matt 25:31–33: when the Son of Man comes in his glory ... he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations. John 5:27: the Father has given him authority to execute judgment, because he is the Son of Man. The Valley of Jehoshaphat (meaning 'YHWH judges') is the OT's prophetic name for the eschatological judgment seat — in the NT, it is the throne of the risen and exalted Christ before whom all nations will stand.
3.3: A shadow: 'And for my people they have cast lots; they have traded a boy for a prostitute, and sold a girl for wine and have drunk it.' The commodification of covenant people — traded, sold, bartered — is the type of Christ's own humiliation. Matt 27:35: they divided his garments among them by casting lots (fulfilling Ps 22:18). Matt 26:15: Judas betrayed him for thirty pieces of silver — Christ sold for the price of a slave (Exod 21:32). The trading of YHWH's people for the cheapest prices (a prostitute, a cup of wine) is the type of the one who was sold and traded at the deepest humiliation — whose price then purchased not wine but the world.
3.4: A shadow: 'But you — what are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia?' YHWH's challenge to the trading nations who had exploited his people finds its NT echo in Christ's challenge to the cities that refused to repent. Matt 11:21–22: woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes. But I tell you, it will be more bearable on the day of judgment for Tyre and Sidon than for you. The trading cities of Joel become the resistant cities of Jesus's Galilean ministry — YHWH's call to account remains the same.
3.5: A shadow: 'For you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.' The plunder of YHWH's treasures — his silver and gold — by the trading nations is the type of every exploitation that will be accounted for in the final judgment. Christ's cleansing of the temple (John 2:13–17; Matt 21:12–13) is the enacted judgment against those who turned the house of prayer into a den of robbers — the same commercialization of what is sacred that Joel condemns. The treasures belong to YHWH and are temporarily taken; the final restoration returns them to their proper owner (Rev 21:24–26: the nations will bring their glory into the new Jerusalem).
3.6: A shadow: 'You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks, removing them far from their own border.' The diaspora — YHWH's people scattered to the ends of the earth by slave traders — is the type of the scattering that Christ came to reverse. John 11:52: Jesus would die to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad. Eph 2:13: you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. The people removed far from their own border are brought back — not merely to a geographic land but to the Father's house (John 14:2–3).
3.7: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I will rouse them from the place to which you sold them, and I will return your payment on your own head.' The divine reversal of exploitation — the scattered gathered, the payment returned to the exploiter — is the justice dimension of the gospel. Luke 1:52–53: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty (Mary's Magnificat). Christ's resurrection is the supreme rousingfrom the deepest 'place to which you sold them' — from the grave itself — and the return of the adversary's payment on his own head (Rev 12:10: the accuser has been thrown down).
3.8: A shadow: 'I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hands of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans, to a nation far away; for the LORD has spoken.' The measure-for-measure reversal of the slave-trading nations is the eschatological justice of Christ's kingdom. Gal 3:28 already removes the slave/free distinction within the covenant community; Rev 18:11–14 describes the final lamentation of the traders whose merchandise is no more — including 'human souls.' The lex talionis of Joel 3:8 — return the payment on the exploiter's head — is the justice embedded in the judgment of Babylon the Great (Rev 18:6: pay her back as she herself has paid back others).
3.9: A shadow: 'Proclaim this among the nations: Prepare for war! Rouse the mighty men! Let all the warriors draw near; let them come up.' The gathering of all nations for the final battle is the OT's template for Rev 16:14 (spirits performing signs who go abroad to the kings of the whole world, to assemble them for battle on the great day of God the Almighty) and Rev 19:19 (the beast and the kings of the earth with their armies gathered to make war against him who was sitting on the horse and against his army). Christ is the one before whom these armies are gathered — and whose word is sufficient to defeat them (Rev 19:21).
3.10: A direct revelation: 'Beat your plowshares into swords and your pruning hooks into spears.' This is the deliberate inversion of the eschatological peace-oracle in Isa 2:4 and Mic 4:3 (they shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks). At the Day of Judgment, before the new creation's peace is established, the judgment must run its course. The sequence: Joel 3:10 (plowshares to swords) comes before Isa 2:4 (swords to plowshares) — judgment precedes and enables the new creation's peace. Christ's parousia in Rev 19:11–21 is the sword that precedes the millennium and the new creation; only after the judgment do the plowshares return.
3.11: A shadow: 'Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there. Bring down your warriors, O LORD.' The gathering of all nations to the valley of judgment is the eschatological assembly. Rev 19:14: the armies of heaven, arrayed in fine linen, white and pure, were following him on white horses. The 'warriors' that YHWH is asked to 'bring down' are the heavenly armies — the angels who accompany the Son of Man at his return (Matt 25:31: the Son of Man will come in his glory, and all the angels with him). Joel's summoning of heavenly warriors is the template for the parousia's angelic host.
3.12: A direct revelation: 'Let the nations be roused and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.' The divine throne in the valley of judgment is the OT's preview of Rev 20:11–12: I saw a great white throne and him who was seated on it ... and I saw the dead, great and small, standing before the throne. John 5:22: the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son. The YHWH who sits to judge all nations in Joel is identified in the NT as the enthroned Christ: Rev 3:21 (the one who conquers, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne).
3.13: A direct revelation: 'Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in and tread, for the winepress is full; the vats overflow, for their wickedness is great.' Revelation 14:14–20 is a direct expansion of this verse: I looked, and behold, a white cloud, and seated on the cloud one like a son of man, with a golden crown on his head, and a sharp sickle in his hand. And another angel called out to him, 'Put in your sickle, and reap, for the hour to reap has come, for the harvest of the earth is fully ripe.' Christ is the Son of Man who wields the harvest sickle and treads the winepress of YHWH's wrath (Rev 19:15). Joel 3:13 is the seed text of Revelation's harvest imagery.
3.14: A direct revelation: 'Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.' The valley of decision is the eschatological moment of universal reckoning — the 'crisis' (Greek krisis = judgment/decision) before which every person stands. John 3:18–19: whoever does not believe is condemned already, because he has not believed in the name of the only Son of God. And this is the judgment (krisis): the light has come into the world, and people loved the darkness rather than the light. The multitudes in Joel's valley are the whole of humanity standing before the One who is himself the Light and the Judge — the valley of decision is wherever Christ is encountered.
3.15: A shadow: 'The sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.' The second occurrence of the cosmic-darkness sign in Joel (cf. 2:10, 31) confirms its eschatological significance. Mark 13:24–25: in those days, after that tribulation, the sun will be darkened and the moon will not give its light, and the stars will be falling from heaven. The withdrawal of stellar light is the removal of the created luminaries — the guides of human navigation — in the face of the uncreated Light who is the Lamb (Rev 21:23: the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb). Creation's lights yield to the uncreated Light.
3.16: A direct revelation: 'The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the heavens and the earth shake. But the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel.' The roaring of YHWH from Zion identifies Christ as the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Rev 5:5). The shaking of heaven and earth (Heb 12:26–27) from the voice of Christ is the final act that establishes the unshakeable kingdom. But the simultaneous terror and shelter — the roaring Judge who is also the refuge — is the gospel in miniature: Heb 12:28–29 (let us be grateful for receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken, and thus let us offer to God acceptable worship with reverence and awe, for our God is a consuming fire).
3.17: A direct revelation: 'So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain. And Jerusalem shall be holy, and strangers shall never again pass through it.' The dwelling of YHWH on Zion is fulfilled in Christ and culminates in the new Jerusalem. John 2:21: he was speaking about the temple of his body. Rev 21:2–3: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God ... the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them. The knowledge of YHWH that Joel promises — the intimate covenantal knowledge of divine presence — is the eternal life that Christ defines: John 17:3 (this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent).
3.18: A direct revelation: 'And in that day the mountains shall drip with sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk, and all the streambeds of Judah shall flow with water, and a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Shittim.' The new-creation abundance — wine from mountains, milk from hills, water from the temple — is the eschatological landscape of Rev 22:1–2 (the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb). The fountain from the house of YHWH is Ezekiel's temple-river (Ezek 47:1–12) and Christ's living water (John 7:38: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water — the Spirit). John 19:34: blood and water came out of the side of the pierced Christ — the fountain from the house of the LORD opened on Calvary.
3.19: A shadow: 'Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness, because of the violence done to the people of Judah, in whose land they shed innocent blood.' The judgment on Egypt and Edom — the historical enemies of Israel — is the type of the final judgment on all hostility to the covenant people. Rev 11:18: the nations raged, but your wrath came, and the time for the dead to be judged, and for rewarding your servants the prophets and saints and those who fear your name, both small and great, and for destroying the destroyers of the earth. The blood of the innocent that cries out from Joel's land (Gen 4:10; Rev 6:10: how long, O Lord, holy and true, before you judge?) is answered in the final judgment.
3.20: A direct revelation: 'But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.' The eternal habitation of Jerusalem is fulfilled in the new Jerusalem. Rev 21:2–4: I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband ... He will dwell with them, and they will be his people ... and death shall be no more. The contrast between Egypt/Edom (desolate) and Judah/Jerusalem (inhabited forever) is the contrast between the perishing and the eternal that runs through the entire NT (John 3:16: everyone who believes should not perish but have eternal life; 2 Cor 4:18: the things that are seen are transient, but the things that are unseen are eternal).
3.21: A direct revelation: 'I will avenge their blood — the blood I have not yet avenged — for the LORD dwells in Zion.' The book's closing verse — YHWH dwelling in Zion as the guarantor of justice for his people's shed blood — is the resolution of Rev 6:10 (the martyrs cry: how long before you will judge and avenge our blood?). Rev 19:2: he has judged the great prostitute ... and has avenged on her the blood of his servants. The dwelling of YHWH in Zion (Joel's closing note) becomes in Rev 21:3 the permanent dwelling of God with his people — the Immanuel promise fulfilled, not merely in a geographic city but in the eternal new creation where God himself is the temple (Rev 21:22: its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb).
